Glossary About
East Asian Traditions

Xunzi

3rd c. BCE

Xunzi, or Xun Kuang 荀況, is one of the most philosophically systematic thinkers of the classical Chinese world.

Xunzi, or Xun Kuang 荀況, is one of the most philosophically systematic thinkers of the classical Chinese world. He lived in the late Warring States period, roughly around 310 BCE and after 238 BCE, and the text transmitted under his name, the Xunzi 荀子, is unusually essayistic, argumentative, and analytically explicit by early Chinese standards. He is normally placed after Confucius and Mencius as one of the major classical Confucian theorists, though later Confucian tradition often treated him as a problematic or heterodox figure because of his famous doctrine that “human nature is bad” or “evil.” Recent scholarship has substantially rehabilitated him as a thinker of ritual, language, psychology, education, political order, and moral transformation.

A philosophy of mind extracted from Xunzi will not look like a Cartesian theory of consciousness, nor like a modern analytic account of propositional attitudes, qualia, or personal identity. His central concern is not “what is the ontological status of the mental?” in isolation. Rather, his central question is: how can a naturally desiring, emotionally reactive, socially interdependent human being become cognitively clear, affectively ordered, ritually competent, and ethically reliable? In that sense, Xunzi’s philosophy of mind is a moral psychology, an epistemology, a theory of affect regulation, a theory of embodied cultivation, and a theory of social cognition at once.

The report below treats Xunzi as a major figure in the global history of philosophy of mind. Its central thesis is this: Xunzi develops a naturalistic, embodied, socially scaffolded, normatively transformative, and executive-control theory of mind. The human mind, or xin 心, is not an immaterial soul; it is the governing heart-mind of an embodied organism. It receives and organizes sensory input, approves and rejects courses of action, regulates desire, corrects cognitive partiality, learns public distinctions through names and ritual, and can be transformed through deliberate artifice, education, music, and ceremonial practice.

1. Historical Position: Xunzi in the Development of Philosophy of Mind

Xunzi belongs to the late Warring States intellectual world, a period of intense competition among Confucians, Mohists, Daoists, Logicians, Legalists, and other masters. By his time, philosophical debate in China had already developed sophisticated problems about human nature, language, moral motivation, social order, ritual practice, epistemic standards, and political authority. Xunzi inherits the Confucian project of moral cultivation but responds to Mencian optimism, Mohist utilitarianism, Daoist spontaneity, sophistic paradox, and proto-Legalist statecraft. The result is a mind-centered Confucianism in which the stability of society depends on the trained discrimination of the heart-mind.

His approximate Western chronological peers are late Plato, Aristotle, the early Hellenistic schools, and later classical debates about reason, passion, habituation, and political order. Aristotle died in 322 BCE; Xunzi was probably born around 310 BCE. This matters because Xunzi is not “pre-philosophical” or merely “ethical” in contrast to allegedly more theoretical Greek psychology. He is working at a comparable level of abstraction, but his guiding problematic is different. Greek philosophers often framed mind through the soul, reason, appetite, perception, and metaphysical form. Xunzi frames mind through xin 心, xing 性, qing 情, yu 欲, wei 偽, li 禮, ming 名, and dao 道: heart-mind, nature, emotion, desire, deliberate artifice, ritual, naming, and Way.

The difference is not that China lacked a philosophy of mind. It is that early Chinese philosophy did not usually isolate “mind” as a self-contained metaphysical substance. The Chinese term xin 心 literally derives from the heart and functions as the nearest classical Chinese equivalent of “mind,” but it includes cognition, emotion, desire, attention, evaluation, and volition. Early Chinese philosophy generally avoids Cartesian ontological dualism: the heart-mind is not an immaterial thinking substance sharply separated from body. It is a psychophysical, embodied, affective-cognitive organ or function.

This gives Xunzi a distinctive place in the global history of philosophy of mind. He is neither a dualist in the Cartesian sense, nor a reductive materialist in the modern neuroscientific sense, nor a behaviorist. He is closer to a naturalistic moral psychologist who treats mind as an embodied executive system shaped by social practice. His account anticipates several themes now familiar in embodied cognition, virtue ethics, social epistemology, affect regulation, cognitive bias theory, and habit formation, though it must not be anachronistically collapsed into any of them.

2. Basic Vocabulary: The Architecture of Xunzian Mind

Any rigorous reconstruction must begin with the core terms.

Xin 心, usually translated “heart-mind,” is the central cognitive-affective-volitional faculty. It knows, approves, rejects, distinguishes, regulates, attends, stores, synthesizes, and commands. Xunzi says the heart-mind is the lord or ruler of the body and the master of its “spirit-like clarity”; it issues commands but does not receive commands in the same way other organs do.

Xing 性, “nature,” means what is given by birth: basic capacities, tendencies, faculties, impulses, and spontaneous dispositions. Xunzi’s controversial thesis that human nature is bad does not mean that human beings are metaphysically damned or incapable of goodness. It means that if native impulses are followed without reflective regulation, ritual form, and social training, they tend toward conflict, contention, and disorder.

Qing 情 refers to the affective, emotional, or dispositional stuff of human life: joy, anger, sorrow, pleasure, aversion, liking, disliking, and the felt texture of response. Xunzi does not propose to eradicate qing. He seeks to shape, express, and regulate it.

Yu 欲, “desire,” is the tendency of affective nature toward objects. Desires are natural and unavoidable. Xunzi explicitly denies that social order depends on eliminating desire. Order depends on whether the heart-mind approves the right standards and regulates pursuit according to ritual and reason.

Wei 偽, often translated “artifice,” “deliberate effort,” or “conscious activity,” is what humans create through learning, reflection, practice, and institutional invention. In Xunzi, “artificial” does not mean fake or corrupt. It means cultivated, made, achieved, and normatively structured. Goodness is not spontaneous nature; it is a human achievement.

Li 禮, ritual, is not mere etiquette. It is a system of embodied norms, social distinctions, emotional forms, ceremonial patterns, role expectations, and symbolic practices that regulate desire and stabilize society. Ritual is a psychological technology as much as a social institution.

Yue 樂, music, is the affective counterpart to ritual. Ritual differentiates; music harmonizes. Music shapes emotion, synchronizes bodies, cultivates shared feeling, and channels joy.

Ming 名, names, are linguistic distinctions. For Xunzi, correct naming is essential to cognition, communication, social order, and governance. Names are conventional, but not arbitrary in the sense of unconstrained; they must track real distinctions in the world and in human practice.

Dao 道, Way, is the normative pattern of proper human life and social order. The heart-mind must know the Way in order to guide conduct. The Way is not merely a private insight. It is embodied in ritual, music, names, institutions, teachers, and the accumulated wisdom of sages.

With these terms in place, Xunzi’s philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as a theory of how a naturally desiring organism becomes a norm-governed person.

3. Naturalism: Heaven, Body, and the Non-Supernatural Mind

Xunzi’s psychology is grounded in his naturalism. “Heaven” or Tian 天 is not a providential deity that rewards virtue and punishes vice. Heaven has constancy. Its processes do not change because of a good ruler like Yao or a bad ruler like Jie. Seasons, stars, weather, and natural regularities operate independently of human moral success. Political order and disorder result from human activity, not divine intervention.

This naturalism is crucial for his philosophy of mind. Xunzi rejects the idea that strange natural events are meaningful supernatural portents. An eclipse, an oddity, or an anomaly may be unusual, but the truly frightening “omens” are human ones: bad agriculture, corrupt government, social disorder, and failed ritual. Ceremonies for rain or divination may have expressive, symbolic, or social value, but they do not magically force Heaven to act.

Mind, then, is not explained by appeal to divine inspiration. Xunzi describes human beings as embodied creatures whose form gives rise to a “spirit” or vital efficacy. The eyes, ears, nose, mouth, and body each have their capacities, while the heart-mind occupies a central governing role, “empty” and capable of directing the five faculties. The sage purifies the heart-mind, rectifies the organs, and nourishes the feelings.

This is neither Cartesian dualism nor modern eliminative materialism. Descartes, much later, makes mind-body dualism a central problem by sharply distinguishing thinking mind from extended body; he even treats the pineal gland as the principal seat of the soul because thought and sensation appear unified. Xunzi has no comparable doctrine of an immaterial thinking substance. But he also does not reduce mind to mere mechanical behavior. The heart-mind is a real functional center of attention, discrimination, approval, and command.

Compared with Aristotle, Xunzi is closer to an embodied account than to a substance dualism. Aristotle treats soul as the form or actuality of a living body and develops psychology within natural philosophy. Xunzi similarly treats mental life as inseparable from embodied faculties, but his main interest is less biological classification than moral-political transformation: how an organism with desires and senses becomes a ritually ordered agent.

4. The Heart-Mind as Executive Governor

The heart-mind is the central organ of Xunzian psychology. Its role is not merely to think, nor merely to feel. It governs. Xunzi repeatedly portrays the body as a polity: the heart-mind is ruler, the sensory organs are subordinate offices, and desires are powerful but governable forces. This analogy is not decorative. It is the structural basis of his psychology. Disorder in the person resembles disorder in the state: subordinate forces usurp command, proper standards are lost, and partial impulses override the whole.

In “Dispelling Blindness” (Jiebi 解蔽), Xunzi states that the heart-mind must know the Way in order to follow the Way. The heart-mind’s proper cognition is not mere factual awareness; it is normative orientation. To know well is to know how to coordinate perception, emotion, desire, and action under the right standard.

The heart-mind performs several functions.

First, it receives sensory information. The senses are “Heaven-endowed offices,” each with its own proper domain: the eyes see, the ears hear, the mouth tastes, the nose smells, the body feels. They produce discriminations of similarity and difference. But sensory organs alone do not yield ordered understanding. They must be governed by the heart-mind.

Second, the heart-mind distinguishes and verifies. Xunzi attributes to the heart-mind a capacity sometimes rendered as verifying cognition or zhengzhi 徵知. It distinguishes reasons, pleasures, emotions, desires, approval, and disapproval. It is the faculty that can grasp relations among sensory inputs and determine whether names, claims, and actions fit reality.

Third, the heart-mind approves and rejects. This is vital. For Xunzi, desire by itself does not determine conduct. People act according to what the heart-mind approves. If desires exceed what is permissible or attainable, the heart-mind can restrain pursuit. If what the heart-mind approves accords with the Way, many desires need not produce disorder. If what it approves is wrong, even few desires can produce disorder.

Fourth, the heart-mind commands the body. Xunzi says the heart-mind issues commands but does not receive them from the other organs in the same way. This does not mean the heart-mind is causally untouched by the body or immune to emotion. It means that in proper agency, the heart-mind is the final executive authority. It can accept what is right and reject what is wrong.

Fifth, the heart-mind can be cultivated. It is not automatically clear. It can be blinded, biased, scattered, agitated, or captured by a single fixation. But through study, ritual, music, teachers, and disciplined attention, it can become empty, unified, and tranquil.

This picture differs sharply from any theory in which mind is a passive mirror of nature. Xunzi’s heart-mind is active, regulative, selective, and normatively responsible. It resembles what modern vocabulary might call an executive-control system, but it is also affective, moral, aesthetic, social, and ritualized.

5. Emptiness, Unity, and Tranquility: Xunzi’s Theory of Cognitive Clarity

One of Xunzi’s most sophisticated contributions to philosophy of mind is his account of how the heart-mind knows the Way. The heart-mind knows by becoming empty (xu 虛), unified (yi 壹), and tranquil or still (jing 靜). These are not mystical states in a purely quietistic sense. They are cognitive virtues.

“Emptiness” does not mean having no knowledge. It means not allowing prior knowledge, assumptions, habits, or fixations to block incoming understanding. The mind can store information while remaining receptive. “Unity” does not mean knowing only one thing. It means not allowing one aspect of a matter to disrupt or distort another; the mind synthesizes without fragmentation. “Tranquility” does not mean inactivity. It means not allowing dreams, fantasies, anxieties, or agitation to disturb clear judgment.

This is a powerful theory of attention and bias. Xunzi’s “blindness” (bi 蔽) is not simple ignorance. It is partial cognition mistaken for total cognition. One sees something real but absolutizes it. Xunzi criticizes rival thinkers precisely this way: Mozi saw utility but was blinded by it; Songzi saw desire but was blinded by it; Shen Buhai saw law or administrative technique but was blinded by it; Zhuangzi saw nature but was blinded by it. Each grasped one corner of the Way and mistook it for the whole.

This gives Xunzi an early and subtle theory of cognitive distortion. Error often arises not because a person sees nothing, but because they see one thing too intensely, without proportion. The problem is not absence of data; it is misordered salience. A healthy heart-mind must be receptive enough to learn, unified enough to synthesize, and calm enough to judge.

In Western comparison, this resembles several traditions without being identical to any of them. Plato treats psychological disorder as a failure of reason to rule appetite and spirit; the just soul is hierarchically ordered. The Stoics later treat passions as involving mistaken judgments or weak suppositions. Xunzi differs from both. He does not divide the soul into Platonic parts with reason, spirit, and appetite as quasi-independent agents. Nor does he identify emotion itself with false judgment. Instead, he sees affect and desire as natural materials that become dangerous when the heart-mind’s standards are partial, agitated, or uncultivated.

6. Human Nature: “Bad” Does Not Mean Hopeless

Xunzi’s most famous thesis is that human nature is bad: ren zhi xing e 人之性惡. This doctrine must be handled carefully. It is often misunderstood, especially when filtered through Christian categories such as sin, depravity, or guilt. Xunzi’s point is not that humans are born morally culpable. Nor is he saying that humans are incapable of goodness. He is saying that the spontaneous tendencies given by birth do not, by themselves, produce ethical order. If people simply follow profit-seeking, resentment, sensory appetite, status desire, and emotional impulse, the result is conflict.

In the “Human Nature is Bad” chapter, Xunzi argues that people are born with desires for profit, aversions to harm, sensory appetites, and emotional reactions. If these are followed without teachers, ritual, law, and righteousness, they lead to contention and disorder. Therefore, goodness is the result of conscious activity, deliberate cultivation, and social instruction.

His analogies are famous: crooked wood must be steamed and bent before it becomes straight; dull metal must be ground before it becomes sharp. In the same way, human beings require teachers, models, ritual principles, and deliberate effort to become morally ordered.

The contrast with Mencius is central. Mencius argues that human nature is good because humans possess incipient moral “sprouts”: compassion, shame, deference, and moral discrimination. Xunzi thinks this mistakes capacity for natural disposition. Humans can become good, but not because fully reliable moral tendencies are already spontaneously present. They become good because the heart-mind can learn, approve norms, and submit desire to ritual form. The two thinkers agree more than the slogan suggests: both think humans can become sages; both think moral cultivation is possible; both think the heart-mind matters. But they disagree about whether morality grows from native moral beginnings or is imposed and internalized through deliberate artifice.

One should also avoid overstating Xunzi as a pure egoist. Some passages suggest that human beings naturally have forms of awareness, attachment, and affection, especially toward kin. Recent scholarship has noted that the Xunzi sometimes portrays humans as affectively complex rather than simply selfish. Blood-and-qi creatures have awareness; humans have special concern for their own kind; feelings for parents are not easily exhausted. The best reconstruction is therefore not “humans are monsters by birth,” but rather: human nature is morally underdetermined and socially dangerous unless shaped by artifice.

7. Desire, Emotion, and Regulation

Xunzi’s theory of desire is among the most philosophically important parts of his mind theory. He does not think desire can be eliminated. He does not even think it should be eliminated. Desire is natural. Even the emperor cannot exhaustively satisfy all desires, and even the poorest person cannot be made desireless. Social order therefore cannot be built on the fantasy of abolishing appetite. It must regulate pursuit.

This makes Xunzi very different from crude asceticism. He does not condemn pleasure as such. Ritual nourishes the senses: mouth, nose, eyes, ears, and body all have legitimate forms of satisfaction. The problem is unbounded seeking. People are born with desires; if they seek satisfaction without limits or distinctions, they contend; contention produces disorder; disorder produces poverty and social breakdown. The ancient kings created ritual and righteousness to establish boundaries, divide roles, regulate desires, and satisfy needs in an ordered way.

The key distinction is between desire and approval. Desire arises from nature. Pursuit is mediated by the heart-mind. The mind can say yes or no. Thus, ethical failure is not the mere presence of desire; it is the heart-mind’s approval of improper pursuit. This is a deep point. Xunzi rejects a simplistic hydraulic model in which desire directly causes action. Instead, he gives a mediated model: nature gives desire; emotion gives urgency; the heart-mind evaluates; action follows approval; ritual and learning shape what the heart-mind approves.

Emotion, too, is not to be eradicated. Mourning rites are a particularly revealing case. Xunzi interprets mourning not as superstition but as the proper form of grief. The three-year mourning period gives sorrow an ordered expression, neither suppressing it nor allowing it to become limitless. Sacrificial rites express longing, remembrance, sorrow, reverence, and loyalty. For the cultivated person, sacrifice is a human ritual form; for common people, it may be entangled with beliefs about spirits, but its philosophical function is to give form to otherwise invisible affective relations.

This is one of Xunzi’s most impressive contributions: he treats ritual as affective form. The goal is not emotional numbness. It is articulate feeling. Grief, reverence, joy, hierarchy, gratitude, and loyalty become psychologically intelligible when enacted through shared forms.

Compared with Stoicism, Xunzi is less suspicious of emotion as such. The Stoics often define passions as mistaken judgments or unstable opinions; Xunzi instead sees emotions as natural responses needing ritual shaping. Compared with Plato, he agrees that unregulated appetite leads to disorder, but he does not treat bodily desire as an alien lower part to be subdued by pure reason. Desire is material for cultivation.

8. Artifice and Transformation: Goodness as an Achievement

Xunzi’s concept of wei 偽 is indispensable. It is often mistranslated in a way that makes Xunzi sound cynical, as if morality were “fake.” But Xunzi’s point is almost the opposite. The genuinely human is not what appears before cultivation; the genuinely excellent human being is the one made through sustained, intelligent, normative effort. Goodness is artificial in the sense that architecture, music, law, scholarship, and ritual are artificial: they are human achievements.

Xunzi distinguishes what cannot be learned or worked at, which belongs to nature, from what can be learned and achieved through effort, which belongs to deliberate artifice. Rites and righteousness are not born in us fully formed; they are created by sages and learned through practice.

This is a philosophy of second nature. The person begins with spontaneous impulses but can acquire stable dispositions. Through repeated practice, one’s responses change. The gentleman studies broadly, examines himself daily, seeks teachers, honors ritual, and accumulates virtue. Just as small steps make a journey and small streams make a river, repeated acts of learning produce clarity and eventually sagely mentality.

Education is not mere information transfer. Xunzi says a moment of genuine learning is better than a whole day of undisciplined thought; the gentleman is not born different from others but is skilled at using external aids. Children may be born with similar cries, but they grow into different customs because of teaching and environment.

This is an early theory of socially mediated cognitive development. The mind is not self-sufficient. It requires teachers, texts, models, ritual forms, and institutions. Xunzi’s cultivated agent is not a solitary Cartesian subject discovering certainty by introspection. He is a learner embedded in a normative community.

The highest achievement is not mechanical conformity. The sage internalizes ritual so deeply that proper action becomes spontaneous. This is not the original spontaneity of untrained nature; it is cultivated spontaneity. The natural person follows impulse; the trained person follows ritual with ease; the sage has transformed the relation between desire, feeling, judgment, and action.

9. Ritual as Cognitive, Affective, and Social Technology

Ritual is the center of Xunzi’s philosophy of mind because it solves the problem generated by nature. Humans have desires. Resources are limited. Status matters. Emotions are intense. Perception is partial. Language can be confused. Without shared form, society collapses into conflict. Ritual provides distinctions, limits, expressive forms, and embodied habits that make stable social cognition possible.

Xunzi’s account of ritual begins from desire and scarcity. People are born with desires; if desires are not bounded, there is contention; if there is contention, there is disorder; if there is disorder, there is poverty. Former kings therefore created ritual and righteousness to divide roles, regulate desires, and align satisfaction with order.

Ritual has several psychological functions.

It trains attention. Ritual tells the person what matters: elder and younger, ruler and minister, parent and child, guest and host, mourning and celebration, reverence and familiarity. It makes social salience public.

It structures desire. Ritual does not abolish wanting. It teaches when, how, toward whom, and in what measure desire may be pursued.

It forms emotion. Mourning ritual articulates grief; sacrificial ritual articulates remembrance; court ritual articulates reverence; marriage ritual articulates union and hierarchy.

It embodies norms. Xunzi does not think moral knowledge lives only as propositions. It is in posture, timing, gesture, dress, music, spatial arrangement, and repetition.

It coordinates society. Ritual makes expectations stable. People can act together because distinctions are shared.

It stabilizes the heart-mind. By repeatedly enacting proper form, the heart-mind acquires reliable patterns of approval and disapproval.

This is why ritual is not reducible to external behavior. In Xunzi, ritual governs the inside by shaping the outside, and it governs the outside by forming the inside. It is a bridge between embodied action and mental order.

Compared with Aristotle, Xunzi shares a commitment to habituation: character is formed through repeated practice, not by abstract theory alone. But Aristotle’s account is organized around virtue, practical wisdom, and the teleology of human flourishing, whereas Xunzi’s is organized around ritual order, teacher-guided transformation, and correction of bad nature. Compared with Confucius, Xunzi is more explicitly psychological and argumentative about why ritual is necessary.

10. Music and the Regulation of Affect

Music is not ornamental in Xunzi. It is a core part of mental formation. If ritual differentiates, music harmonizes. Xunzi says music arises from joy, and joy cannot fail to be expressed. When joy is expressed without guidance, it can become disorderly; when guided by the Way, it harmonizes people and transforms customs.

Music affects the heart-mind quickly and deeply. Proper music harmonizes qi, clears the mind, dignifies demeanor, and aligns social ranks. Corrupt music produces licentiousness, disorder, and emotional disarray. Mourning music brings sorrow; dignified music produces reverence; licentious music arouses lust.

This resembles Plato’s concern in the Republic that music and education shape the soul. Plato’s psychology famously divides the soul into reason, spirit, and appetite, and political education seeks to harmonize these parts. Xunzi’s parallel is striking: both thinkers see music as morally and politically consequential because it forms affective dispositions before explicit reasoning fully operates. But Xunzi’s framework is not metaphysical tripartition. It is ritual-affective governance by the heart-mind.

Music shows that Xunzi’s mind is not merely cognitive in the narrow sense. Human mentality is rhythmic, affective, bodily, and socially contagious. People are transformed not only by arguments but by sounds, ceremonies, movements, and shared emotional atmospheres.

11. Language, Naming, and Social Cognition

Xunzi’s philosophy of language is also a philosophy of mind. In the “Rectification of Names” (Zhengming 正名), he argues that when names and realities are confused, intentions are not understood and affairs fail. Wise rulers establish names to indicate realities, clarify similarities and differences, distinguish ranks, and make communication effective.

Names are conventional. Xunzi explicitly says names have no inherent appropriateness; they become appropriate by communal agreement and usage. Yet he is not a relativist. Different realities should receive different names, and same realities should receive the same name. Naming must be answerable to distinctions in things and practices.

This position is subtle: semantic conventionalism plus realist constraint. The sound or written form of a name is assigned by convention, but successful naming depends on stable discrimination of similarities and differences. The sensory organs and heart-mind provide the cognitive basis for such discrimination. The heart-mind distinguishes emotions, reasons, pleasures, desires, and categories; names then stabilize these distinctions publicly.

The mental significance is enormous. Language is not merely a label pasted onto private thought. It is a public technology for organizing cognition. If names are corrupted, the heart-mind’s ability to deliberate, communicate, and govern is weakened. Social disorder is partly semantic disorder.

This makes Xunzi relevant to later philosophy of mind and language. He rejects the view that meaning is purely natural and inherent; he also rejects the view that naming is arbitrary in a way unconstrained by the world. Names are instituted, but they must enable successful discrimination, inference, governance, and action.

In Western comparison, one might contrast this with Plato’s concern in dialogues such as the Cratylus about whether names are natural or conventional, and with later nominalist and conventionalist theories. Xunzi’s distinctive feature is that naming is politically and psychologically urgent. Confused names do not merely produce bad metaphysics; they produce failed government, corrupt roles, and disordered minds.

12. Bias, Blindness, and the Pathology of One-Sidedness

Xunzi’s concept of bi 蔽, “blindness” or “obscuration,” is one of his deepest psychological ideas. Blindness is not simply error. It is the narrowing of the heart-mind by fixation. A person grasps one valid consideration and lets it block others. Utility, desire, law, nature, rhetoric, or spontaneity may each reveal something real; each becomes dangerous when absolutized.

This is why Xunzi criticizes other philosophical schools as one-sided. Mozi is blinded by utility; Zhuangzi is blinded by nature; other thinkers are blinded by law, power, or linguistic subtlety. The sage is not someone who ignores these partial insights. He is someone whose heart-mind is not captured by them. He holds the central balance and sees the whole.

This anticipates a broad family of later ideas: cognitive bias, attentional capture, motivated reasoning, ideological fixation, and professional deformation. Xunzi’s account is not empirical psychology in the modern experimental sense, but it is philosophically precise. The problem of mind is not only passion overpowering reason. It is reason itself becoming partial.

This also complicates the common contrast between “emotion” and “rationality.” In Xunzi, a mind can be distorted by desire, but also by doctrine, abstraction, utility, anti-conventionalism, or rhetoric. The cure is not cold cognition alone. The cure is disciplined receptivity, synthetic unity, tranquility, teachers, ritual, music, and the Way.

13. Agency: Approval, Weighing, and Responsibility

Xunzi has a sophisticated account of agency. Human beings do not simply act from desire. They act from what the heart-mind approves after some form of weighing. The terms of approval and rejection are central. People pursue what they approve and avoid what they disapprove; the issue is whether their standards of approval are aligned with the Way.

This gives Xunzi an account of responsibility. If desire were irresistible, moral education would be impossible. If goodness were innate and spontaneous, ritual transformation would be unnecessary. Xunzi occupies the middle ground: desire is natural and powerful, but the heart-mind can regulate pursuit through learned standards. People are responsible because the heart-mind can learn to approve rightly.

He also emphasizes weighing, discretion, and context. Proper action is not rigid rule-following in the crude sense. One must know what matters more and less, what is fitting, what accords with ritual, and how distinctions apply. This again makes the heart-mind a practical faculty, not merely a theoretical one.

The cultivated agent is therefore not desireless. He is someone whose desires, emotions, and actions are governed by a stable hierarchy of approval. In the sage, this hierarchy is so internalized that action becomes effortless. Xunzi can thus explain both moral struggle and moral ease: early cultivation requires effort against impulse; mature cultivation produces integrated spontaneity.

14. The Social Mind: Why Mind Is Not Private

Xunzi’s heart-mind is individual, but not individualistic. Its formation depends on public practices. Teachers, ritual, music, names, law, models, texts, and institutions shape what the heart-mind can perceive, desire, approve, and enact. A mind outside social form is not a pure rational subject; it is an untrained field of impulses and partial perceptions.

This makes Xunzi especially important for a social philosophy of mind. He does not treat cognition as merely internal representation. Cognition is scaffolded by public forms. The person learns distinctions through ritual, stabilizes categories through names, regulates affect through music, and acquires standards through teachers. Even self-control is socially mediated.

Modern embodied and extended cognition theories often challenge the idea that cognition is confined to computation inside the brain. Contemporary embodied cognition research likewise contrasts with older models that treated cognition as primarily computational processing located in the brain. Xunzi should not be called a modern embodied cognition theorist without qualification, but his view has clear structural affinities: mental order is achieved through bodily practice, artifacts, social norms, and environmental structuring.

This is also why Xunzi’s philosophy of mind is inseparable from politics. If the heart-mind is formed by public practices, then political disorder is also psychological disorder. Bad rulers, corrupt names, degraded music, improper ritual, and poor education deform minds. Good government is therefore a condition of mental cultivation, not merely an external arrangement.

15. Self-Cultivation: From Raw Nature to Sagehood

Xunzi’s developmental psychology can be reconstructed in stages.

The first stage is raw nature. The person has senses, desires, emotions, aversions, and basic capacities. These are not evil in the sense of demonic, but they are disorder-producing if unregulated.

The second stage is instruction. The learner encounters teachers, texts, models, and ritual. External standards begin to discipline internal approval.

The third stage is practice. Ritual and learning are repeated. The body becomes trained; perception becomes refined; emotional expression becomes structured; desire is moderated.

The fourth stage is integration. The heart-mind becomes more empty, unified, and tranquil. It is less captured by partiality and more able to weigh circumstances.

The fifth stage is gentlemanly reliability. The cultivated person acts according to ritual even under pressure. Profit, power, and sensory temptation no longer easily sway him.

The final stage is sagehood. The sage embodies the Way with ease. Proper action no longer feels like external constraint because ritual has become second nature.

This developmental model depends on accumulation. Xunzi repeatedly stresses that learning must not stop. Accumulated effort produces transformation just as accumulated steps make a journey and accumulated streams form a river.

The philosophical significance is that Xunzi rejects both nativist moral optimism and mere coercive externalism. He does not think goodness is simply born within us; but he also does not think morality is only external obedience. Cultivation changes the structure of the heart-mind.

16. Xunzi, Mencius, and the Confucian Philosophy of Mind

The contrast with Mencius is the most important internal Chinese comparison. Mencius argues that human nature is good and that the heart-mind possesses moral sprouts. Xunzi argues that human nature is bad and that goodness comes from deliberate artifice. But their disagreement is subtler than slogans imply.

Mencius’s model is developmental actualization: moral tendencies are already present in seed form and must be nurtured. Xunzi’s model is transformative construction: moral order is not naturally present as a reliable tendency but can be built through ritual, learning, and conscious activity.

Mencius tends to trust spontaneous moral affect, especially compassion. Xunzi distrusts spontaneity unless it has been cultivated. Mencius fears losing the original heart. Xunzi fears mistaking raw impulse for the Way. Mencius’s paradigm is agricultural growth; Xunzi’s is craft transformation: bending wood, sharpening metal, training the body, rectifying names.

Yet both are Confucian. Both reject the idea that human beings are fixed in vice. Both place the heart-mind at the center. Both see moral life as cultivated through practice. Both care about ritual, though Xunzi gives ritual a more architectonic role. The deepest disagreement is over the source of normativity: is morality an unfolding of native tendencies, or is it an artifice created by sages and internalized through disciplined practice?

In the larger history of philosophy of mind, this is a major debate about moral psychology: is ethical cognition innate, constructed, or some combination of both? Xunzi’s answer is constructivist but not relativist, naturalist but not reductionist, social but not behaviorist.

17. Xunzi and Plato

The comparison with Plato is illuminating. Both Plato and Xunzi see disorder as a failure of psychic governance. Plato’s Republic famously divides the soul into reason, spirit, and appetite; justice occurs when reason rules, spirit supports reason, and appetite obeys. Xunzi similarly thinks desire must be governed by a higher faculty, the heart-mind, and he uses political metaphors to describe psychic order.

Both also treat music and education as psychologically formative. Plato worries that musical modes, poetry, and bodily training shape the soul before rational argument fully matures. Xunzi likewise thinks music and ritual transform emotion and conduct.

The differences are equally important. Plato’s psychology is tied to metaphysics: Forms, immortality of soul in several dialogues, and the superiority of philosophical reason. Xunzi has no theory of transcendent Forms and no central doctrine of an immortal soul. His Way is not a metaphysical object outside the human world; it is a normatively authoritative pattern embodied in ritual, music, names, and institutions.

Plato often treats appetite as a lower psychic part requiring rule by reason. Xunzi treats desire as natural material requiring regulation through ritual. He is less interested in separating psychic parts and more interested in training the heart-mind’s approvals. Plato’s soul is internally plural; Xunzi’s mind is functionally hierarchical.

18. Xunzi and Aristotle

Aristotle and Xunzi converge around habituation, practical formation, and the importance of education. Aristotle’s virtue ethics emphasizes that character is formed through repeated action; Xunzi’s self-cultivation likewise depends on repeated learning, ritual practice, and accumulated effort. Aristotle’s psychology treats the soul as the form of a living body, not as a Cartesian immaterial substance; Xunzi likewise embeds mind in bodily life.

But Aristotle is more teleological in his account of human nature. Human beings have a natural function, and virtue is the excellent fulfillment of that nature. Xunzi is more suspicious of nature as a moral guide. For him, nature supplies capacities and desires, but not reliable ethical form. Goodness is achieved by artifice, not by simply actualizing native tendencies.

Aristotle’s habituation cultivates what is in some sense naturally appropriate to rational animals. Xunzi’s cultivation corrects and transforms what is naturally dangerous in desiring animals. This gives Xunzi a more anti-naturalist moral psychology, even though his ontology is naturalistic.

19. Xunzi and the Stoics

The Stoics and Xunzi both care deeply about the governance of the mind. Both think disorder arises when the mind assents wrongly or is carried away by improper evaluative structures. The Stoics define passions as involving judgments or opinions, often false or unstable ones. Xunzi similarly thinks action depends on what the heart-mind approves.

But Xunzi’s relation to emotion is less eliminative. He does not aim at the eradication of grief, joy, reverence, or desire. He wants them expressed in proper form. Mourning rituals do not teach one not to grieve; they teach one how to grieve. Music does not suppress joy; it harmonizes joy.

The Stoic ideal often emphasizes inner discipline against external fortune. Xunzi emphasizes social and ritual formation. A Stoic sage may remain ordered even in a corrupt society; Xunzi’s sage is deeply dependent on inherited ritual forms and the institutions of civilization. Xunzi’s mind is more socially embedded.

20. Xunzi and Hobbes

Xunzi is sometimes compared with Hobbes because both see unregulated human desire as producing conflict and both think artificial order is necessary. The comparison is useful but limited. Hobbes begins from human passions, language, and individuals in order to explain political order; Xunzi likewise moves from desire and contention to the need for ritual, law, and rulership.

The difference is that Hobbesian order is often framed through covenant, sovereignty, fear of death, and rational self-preservation. Xunzian order is ritual-moral and transformative. Xunzi does not merely ask how mutually threatening individuals can be restrained. He asks how people can be educated into reverence, hierarchy, affective propriety, and cultivated judgment. His solution is not only coercive state power but ritual civilization.

Xunzi is therefore not simply “the Chinese Hobbes.” His anthropology is darker than Mencius’s, but his political psychology is more perfectionist than Hobbes’s. He wants not only peace, but cultivated persons.

21. Xunzi and Descartes

The contrast with Descartes marks a major divergence between classical Chinese and early modern European philosophy of mind. Descartes makes the mind-body problem central by distinguishing thinking substance from extended substance. The modern philosophical problem of consciousness is deeply shaped by this dualist inheritance.

Xunzi does not begin from the certainty of private consciousness. He does not ask how an inner subject can know an external world. He does not treat mind as transparent to itself. Indeed, his theory of blindness suggests the heart-mind is often opaque, partial, and self-deceived. The problem is not escaping from subjective interiority; the problem is training a socially embodied heart-mind to see and approve rightly.

Nor does Xunzi sharply separate cognition from affect. The heart-mind distinguishes reasons and emotions, but both belong to its field of operation. There is no pure intellect detached from embodied desire. Xunzi’s mind is psychophysical, affective, evaluative, and practical.

For this reason, Xunzi sidesteps many modern mind-body puzzles but raises others. He does not give a theory of phenomenal consciousness, qualia, or neural realization. His interest is not “what makes experience subjective?” but “what makes agency ordered?” A modern reader looking for consciousness theory in the narrow sense may be disappointed. A reader looking for moral-cognitive architecture will find an unusually rich account.

22. Xunzi and Modern Philosophy of Mind

Xunzi is not a modern cognitive scientist, but several of his ideas resonate with contemporary discussions.

First, his theory of bi, blindness, resembles cognitive bias theory. Agents are not merely ignorant; they are selectively captured by partial salience.

Second, his account of ritual and music resembles embodied and affective cognition. Mental states are shaped by posture, sound, repetition, environment, and social coordination.

Third, his theory of names anticipates social externalism about cognition. Public language organizes thought and action.

Fourth, his idea that the heart-mind governs approval resembles executive-control models of self-regulation.

Fifth, his emphasis on teachers, models, and external aids resembles scaffolded cognition. The gentleman is not born different; he is skilled at using tools, models, and institutions.

But Xunzi should not be assimilated too quickly to modern theories. He does not have neuroscience, computational modeling, experimental psychology, or liberal individualism. His framework is hierarchical, ritualist, perfectionist, and deeply political. The mind is to be cultivated into a historically inherited normative order, not simply freed for autonomous preference satisfaction.

23. The Comprehensive Xunzian Philosophy of Mind: Twelve Theses

A systematic extraction of Xunzi’s philosophy of mind can be stated as twelve theses.

1. The mind is embodied. The heart-mind is not an immaterial soul detached from the body. It is the central governing function of an organism with eyes, ears, mouth, nose, body, emotions, desires, and vital energies.

2. The mind is executive. The heart-mind governs the body, regulates the senses, approves and rejects desires, and directs action. It is the ruler of the person.

3. Human nature is morally insufficient. Native desires and emotions are not reliable guides to order. If followed without ritual and reflection, they lead to contention and disorder.

4. Desire is natural, not eliminable. The problem is not the existence of desire but unregulated pursuit. The mind must govern desire by standards.

5. Action is mediated by approval. People act according to what the heart-mind approves. Therefore moral transformation must reshape standards of approval, not merely suppress impulses.

6. Goodness is artificial in the noble sense. Virtue is produced through deliberate effort, learning, teachers, ritual, and accumulated practice. It is made, not merely found.

7. Cognitive clarity requires emptiness, unity, and tranquility. The heart-mind must remain receptive, integrative, and undisturbed by fixation or fantasy.

8. Error is often partiality. The mind becomes blind when one valid consideration blocks the whole. Philosophical doctrines themselves can become cognitive pathologies.

9. Language structures thought. Correct names stabilize public distinctions and enable proper intention, communication, governance, and cognition. Names are conventional but reality-constrained.

10. Ritual forms emotion. Ritual does not merely constrain external behavior; it gives shape to grief, reverence, desire, hierarchy, and social expectation.

11. Music harmonizes affect. Music transforms the heart-mind by shaping joy, sorrow, reverence, and shared emotional atmosphere.

12. Mind is socially scaffolded. The cultivated mind is produced through teachers, models, names, ritual, music, and institutions. Mental order is inseparable from civilization.

Together, these theses yield a complete Xunzian philosophy of mind: the mind is an embodied executive faculty whose natural materials are desire and emotion, whose dangers are partiality and disorder, whose tools are naming, ritual, music, and learning, and whose achievement is cultivated alignment with the Way.

24. Tensions and Problems in Xunzi’s Theory

A rigorous treatment must also identify the tensions.

The first tension concerns human nature. If human nature is bad, how can the desire to become good arise? Xunzi answers that people desire goodness because their nature is bad: they lack order and therefore seek what they lack. But this answer may seem unstable. If the heart-mind has enough capacity to recognize goodness, then nature cannot be wholly bad in every respect. Some scholars therefore interpret Xunzi’s “bad nature” thesis as meaning not that every natural tendency is vicious, but that untrained nature is normatively unreliable and socially disordering.

The second tension concerns creation and discovery. Are rituals created by sages, or discovered from objective patterns? Xunzi says sages create ritual, but they do so in response to constant facts: desire, scarcity, hierarchy, kinship, emotion, and social interdependence. The best interpretation is that ritual is constructed but not arbitrary. It is an artifice constrained by human nature and social reality.

The third tension concerns authority. Xunzi relies heavily on sages, teachers, ancient kings, and ritual tradition. This gives his theory strong resources for education but weak resources for dissent. If the heart-mind is trained by existing institutions, how does one criticize corrupt institutions? Xunzi can answer through the Way, proper names, and the standards of ritual, but his theory remains vulnerable to authoritarian appropriation.

The fourth tension concerns hierarchy. Ritual clarifies distinctions of rank, age, role, and status. Xunzi thinks such distinctions prevent conflict and regulate desire. Modern egalitarian readers may object that ritual hierarchy can stabilize domination. This is not an accidental issue; hierarchy is central to Xunzi’s social psychology. A modern appropriation of Xunzi must decide whether ritual distinction can be reconstructed without inherited status inequality.

The fifth tension concerns inner transformation versus external conformity. Does ritual truly transform the heart-mind, or merely discipline behavior? Xunzi clearly intends transformation: ritual, music, and learning reshape approval, emotion, and desire. But any ritualist theory risks producing performers who conform outwardly without inward clarity. Xunzi’s emphasis on the heart-mind’s emptiness, unity, tranquility, and genuine approval is meant to avoid this problem.

The sixth tension concerns naturalism and normativity. Xunzi’s Heaven is morally indifferent. If nature supplies no moral order, where does normativity come from? Xunzi’s answer is that normativity comes from the Way created and transmitted by sages in response to human conditions. This is neither divine command nor natural teleology. It is a form of ritual humanism.

25. Why Xunzi Matters for Philosophy of Mind

Xunzi matters because he offers a major alternative to several dominant Western framings.

Against Cartesianism, he shows how to theorize mind without beginning from isolated inner consciousness.

Against crude materialism, he preserves the reality of evaluation, attention, self-cultivation, and normativity.

Against sentimental moral psychology, he warns that spontaneous feeling is unreliable.

Against rationalism, he insists that cognition is embodied, musical, ritual, and affective.

Against behaviorism, he gives the heart-mind a central internal role.

Against individualism, he shows that mind is formed by public names, teachers, ceremonies, and institutions.

Against relativism, he argues that convention can be objective enough to govern life when constrained by human realities.

His philosophy of mind is therefore not a minor appendix to his ethics. It is the foundation of his entire system. Human beings are not naturally ordered; order must be made. But order can be made because the heart-mind has the capacity to learn, distinguish, approve, and transform itself through practice.

26. Final Synthesis

Xunzi’s philosophy of mind can be summarized in one sentence:

The human heart-mind is an embodied, affective-cognitive governor that begins amid unruly natural desires, becomes distorted by partiality, and achieves clarity and virtue only through deliberate cultivation by ritual, music, language, teachers, and social order.

This is a remarkably comprehensive theory. It includes an ontology of mind, a faculty psychology, a theory of desire, an account of emotion, a model of agency, an epistemology of bias and clarity, a philosophy of language, a developmental psychology, a theory of embodied practice, and a social theory of mental formation.

In the broader history of philosophy of mind, Xunzi deserves to be read alongside Plato, Aristotle, the Stoics, Hobbes, and modern embodied cognition theorists—not because he says the same things, but because he asks a question many later traditions neglect: what kind of social, ritual, linguistic, and affective formation must a mind undergo before it can think and act well?

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
